labels.draftLabel: Archive-inspired generated draft, not a historical article.
labels.articleLabel: Archive-inspired generated article. This is not an original historical news article.
labels.factNotice: Facts come from the selected story fragment. Time and tone only change the editorial voice.
labels.waitingNotice: Awaiting send to newsroom — not published until typewriter key is pressed.

subjects.title: The citizens
subjects.draftText: Editorial focus on people who gather, pass through, and give meaning to shared space.
subjects.generationHint: Frame as a collective urban presence—not as named individuals performing the event.

subjects.title: Chalmers students
subjects.draftText: Focus on student traditions and how they temporarily reshape public visibility in the city.
subjects.generationHint: Treat students as a cultural group in the city, not as the literal organisers of every action in the fragment.

subjects.title: Local journalists
subjects.draftText: Focus on who decides what becomes visible as local news and how the desk frames the story.
subjects.generationHint: Emphasise editorial process and public visibility, not insider biography.

subjects.title: Tourists
subjects.draftText: Focus on how visitors read monuments, signs, and fragments of local history.
subjects.generationHint: Use visitor perspective without stereotyping or mockery.

subjects.title: City officials
subjects.draftText: Focus on responsibility for how places are maintained, named, and used.
subjects.generationHint: Discuss civic responsibility without inventing specific officials or accusations.

subjects.title: The city
subjects.draftText: Focus on Gothenburg as a living editorial subject—streets, institutions, everyday rhythm.
subjects.generationHint: Personify the city cautiously; avoid claiming the city literally acts.

subjects.title: Forgotten voices
subjects.draftText: Focus on people and stories at the margin of archive and public memory.
subjects.generationHint: Signal absence and omission without inventing identifiable victims.

locations.title: Harry Hjörnes Plats
locations.draftText: Scene: a square where journalism, city memory, and everyday movement meet.
locations.generationHint: Anchor the report in this named square as civic and media memory.

locations.title: The monument
locations.draftText: Scene: a fixed monument around which memory is read and questioned.
locations.generationHint: Treat the monument as a symbolic anchor, not a verified historical document.

locations.title: Chalmers campus
locations.draftText: Scene: a student environment where traditions and public rituals often begin.
locations.generationHint: Situate student culture without claiming official university statements.

locations.title: The Cortège route
locations.draftText: Scene: streets temporarily turned into a moving public stage.
locations.generationHint: Describe movement through the city without precise route claims.

locations.title: A public square
locations.draftText: Scene: open ground where strangers briefly share the same story.

locations.title: The riverside
locations.draftText: Scene: waterfront where movement, leisure, and public life become visible.
locations.generationHint: Use waterfront atmosphere without specific event claims.

locations.title: A tram stop
locations.draftText: Scene: a daily waiting place where urban rhythms briefly align.
locations.generationHint: Keep scale modest—everyday life, not spectacle.

locations.title: The newsroom desk
locations.draftText: Scene: symbolic editing space where fragments are selected and framed.
locations.generationHint: Frame as editorial process, not a real GP newsroom transcript.

locations.title: A city festival stage
locations.draftText: Scene: temporary stage where music and attention reshape the moment.
locations.generationHint: Suggest festival energy without naming real performers.

locations.title: A forgotten corner
locations.draftText: Scene: a quiet place where stories sit outside the usual spotlight.
locations.generationHint: Signal marginality without inventing hidden crimes.

times.title: 1920s–1930s
times.subtitle: Hjörne's Newsroom
times.draftText: Voice filter: early local press—formal, civic, confident in public order.
times.styleHint: Use a formal, confident, early newspaper voice. Emphasise order, civic progress, and local news authority.

times.title: 1940s–1950s
times.subtitle: Civic Authority
times.draftText: Voice filter: institutional seriousness and shared civic responsibility.
times.styleHint: Use a serious, orderly tone. Emphasise stability, institutions, and public duty.

times.title: 1960s–1970s
times.subtitle: Public Debate
times.draftText: Voice filter: socially aware debate and collective questions in public life.
times.styleHint: Use a debate-oriented tone. Emphasise participation, responsibility, and open questions.

times.title: 1980s–1990s
times.subtitle: Local City Culture
times.draftText: Voice filter: lively local culture, crowds, students, everyday city events.
times.styleHint: Use a lively local newspaper voice. Emphasise city events, youth culture, and public life.

times.title: 2000s–2010s
times.subtitle: Digital Transition
times.draftText: Voice filter: print meeting online—updates, visibility, changing reader habits.
times.styleHint: Use a hybrid print-and-digital tone. Emphasise circulation, reaction, and media change.

times.title: Today
times.subtitle: Fragmented Media
times.draftText: Voice filter: fragmented attention, multiple perspectives, fast circulation.
times.styleHint: Use a contemporary, reflective, slightly fragmented media voice. Emphasise visibility and uncertainty.

tones.title: Nostalgic
tones.draftText: Stance: warm memory and what the city chooses to keep.
tones.styleHint: Use a warm, reflective tone focused on continuity and remembrance.
tones.doNot: Do not invent sentimental backstories or false personal histories.

tones.title: Critical
tones.draftText: Stance: questions of power, exclusion, and unresolved public responsibility.
tones.styleHint: Use an analytical tone focused on debate and responsibility.
tones.doNot: Do not state unverified accusations as established fact.

tones.title: Civic
tones.draftText: Stance: shared space, participation, and collective urban meaning.
tones.doNot: Do not flatten conflict into vague harmony.

tones.title: Humorous
tones.draftText: Stance: irony and the slightly absurd side of public life.
tones.styleHint: Use playful newspaper irony without becoming unserious reportage.
tones.doNot: Do not mock real groups, disasters, suffering, or identifiable individuals.

tones.title: Suspicious
tones.draftText: Stance: cautious questions—what the visible story may not show.
tones.styleHint: Use investigative caution and open questions.
tones.doNot: Do not invent conspiracy claims, criminal accusations, or hoaxes.

tones.title: Celebratory
tones.draftText: Stance: shared joy, ritual, and festive public gathering.
tones.styleHint: Use an uplifting tone focused on togetherness and seasonal energy.
tones.doNot: Do not ignore tension where the fragment implies debate or harm.

storyFragments.title: Chalmers Cortège enters the city
storyFragments.draftText: A student parade turns streets into temporary public spectacle.
storyFragments.baseFact: Chalmers Cortège is a student parade tradition in Gothenburg, known for humour, handmade floats, and public performance.
storyFragments.storyAngle: How the city watches student humour and public spectacle.
headlineSeeds: When Student Humour Turns the Street Into a Stage
storyFragments.editorNote: Parade fragment—subject is editorial lens, not parade organiser.

storyFragments.title: Valborg welcomes spring in public
storyFragments.draftText: Spring ritual gathers the city around songs and shared seasonal markers.
storyFragments.baseFact: Valborg in Sweden marks spring with public gatherings, songs, and bonfires that turn ordinary places into ritual space.
storyFragments.storyAngle: Seasonal welcome as a moment of shared civic visibility.
storyFragments.editorNote: Seasonal fragment—keep facts ritual, not weather forecast.

storyFragments.title: Midsummer gathers the city outdoors
storyFragments.draftText: Summer ritual makes seasonal life visible in parks and waterfronts.
storyFragments.baseFact: Midsummer in Sweden brings flowers, songs, and gatherings that make seasonal ritual visible in public space.
storyFragments.storyAngle: Summer leisure read through public ritual and city image.
headlineSeeds: A Summer Ritual Meets the City
storyFragments.editorNote: Summer fragment—avoid inventing specific performers or venues beyond the scene.

storyFragments.title: Lucia carries light through winter streets
storyFragments.draftText: Winter procession brings songs and symbolic light into darker months.
storyFragments.baseFact: Lucia processions bring songs and candlelight into winter streets, turning symbolic light into a shared civic image.
storyFragments.storyAngle: Symbolic light as winter public imagery.
storyFragments.editorNote: Procession fragment—focus on symbolism, not religious doctrine.

storyFragments.title: A public square becomes shared ground
storyFragments.draftText: An open square holds gathering, expression, and public attention.
storyFragments.baseFact: Public squares in Gothenburg function as shared ground where citizens, officials, and media align around visible events.
storyFragments.storyAngle: Shared ground and who is allowed to be seen there.
storyFragments.editorNote: Square fragment—do not imply illegal activity without evidence.

storyFragments.title: A monument prompts public memory debate
storyFragments.draftText: A monument becomes a fixed point for reading and questioning memory.
storyFragments.baseFact: Stone monuments act as fixed points around which public memory is read, questioned, and sometimes rewritten.
storyFragments.storyAngle: What the city chooses to remember and what it debates.
storyFragments.editorNote: Monument fragment—question memory, do not invent monument history.

storyFragments.title: A forgotten story returns to the desk
storyFragments.draftText: An overlooked story re-enters attention through a local news frame.
storyFragments.baseFact: Newsrooms sometimes return overlooked stories—fragments never fully published, or voices at the archive's edge.
storyFragments.storyAngle: Return of absence to the editorial desk.
storyFragments.editorNote: Archive fragment—do not invent named victims or scandals.

storyFragments.title: A photograph turns the city into evidence
storyFragments.draftText: A photograph makes an ordinary moment reportable, archivable, or questionable.
storyFragments.baseFact: A single photograph can turn an ordinary city moment into evidence—a frame treated as reportable reality.
storyFragments.storyAngle: The image as editorial evidence, not truth on its own.
headlineSeeds: When a Photograph Enters the Record
The City in a Single Frame
storyFragments.editorNote: Photo fragment—do not claim the image shows crime or guilt.

bodyTemplates: {baseFact} This archive-inspired dispatch places {subjectTitle} at the centre of the story and situates it at {locationTitle}. The {timeTitle} filter shapes the newspaper voice, while the {toneTitle} stance changes what the article chooses to emphasise.
{baseFact} {storyAngle} The report centres on {subjectTitle} at {locationTitle}. The {timeTitle} period filter adjusts language only—not a claim of verified archive fact.
